intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Deep Agents CLI (`deepagents-cli`) is a deterministic, one-shot local developer command-line tool that scaffolds, validates, bundles, and deploys a Managed Deep Agents project onto the LangSmith platform. It performs no LLM inference itself; it assembles an agent payload and managed-directory tree purely from a project's declared sources (`agent.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `tools.json`, `skills/`, `subagents/`) and upserts it over HTTPS with the developer's own API key, and it manages the workspace agents and MCP servers that project references. Its central promise is bundle fidelity — the deployed artifact must reflect only the developer's declared, reviewed project — backed by HTTPS-only transport, confirmation gates on remote-agent updates and deletions, and a refusal to auto-register MCP servers. Referenced remote MCP server URLs are declared to require TLS, and platform/provider/Hub credentials must never be logged, printed, committed, or folded into the bundle.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python 3.11+ package (`deepagents_cli`) built with hatchling, exposing an argparse CLI (`main.py`) with a closed four-command dispatch table — `init`, `deploy`, `agents`, `mcp-servers` — where a bare invocation prints a redirect to `deepagents-code` and exits non-zero. The deploy pipeline lives under `deepagents_cli/deploy/`: `project.py` parses and validates the on-disk project with strict path containment and symlink rejection, `payload.py` is a pure function that emits only managed paths, `api_client.py` wraps `httpx.Client(trust_env=False)` against an HTTPS-forced endpoint, `mcp_resolver.py` validates referenced MCP servers against the live workspace list, and `commands.py` wires the interactive confirmation gates. There is no model client, no subprocess shell, and no REPL. The one material divergence from the remit: no code path validates that a referenced MCP server URL uses TLS — only the platform endpoint is scheme-checked.

behavior_summary: This is a well-bounded deterministic deploy tool whose core trust-boundary controls are real and code-enforced: HTTPS-only transport with userinfo and proxy-env rejection, strict project-root path containment with symlink rejection on every read, interactive confirmation gates for remote-agent updates and resource deletion, and MCP auth-header redaction — several of them locked in by targeted security regression tests. The findings are largely independent rather than a single chain (the tool has no LLM, no exec, and no external-input-to-action path, so no compound escalation applies). The one control that diverges from a declared "Never Allowed" boundary is transport security for MCP servers: the CLI enforces TLS on the platform endpoint but performs no scheme validation on the `mcp_server_url`s it registers, references, and deploys — the remit's TLS-for-MCP requirement has no enforcement point in `mcp_resolver.py`, `commands.py`, or `project.py`. The remaining themes are posture gaps — floor-pinned direct dependencies (mitigated by a committed lockfile), a stale auto-generated threat model, and no durable audit trail of mutating actions.

rules.rule_text: Shipping a bundle assembled from anything other than the project's declared sources.
Deploying a project that failed configuration validation.
Auto-registering an MCP server, or deploying while a referenced MCP server is unregistered or uninvocable by the caller's identity.
Offer a --dry-run that prints the exact payload and directory files it would send, without contacting any mutating endpoint.
Reaching the platform over a non-HTTPS endpoint, or one carrying userinfo.
Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (http:// / ws://) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (https / wss).
Writing, committing to version control, logging, or printing platform / provider / Hub / tracing credentials or MCP auth-header values.
Pulling any file into the bundle from outside the resolved project root, via symlink, path traversal, or an undeclared source.
Creating a fresh agent via --reset while agent.json still declares an agent_id.

rules.section: Action Boundaries → Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Deploying to an agent_id declared in agent.json that local state has not previously confirmed — the CLI must show the target agent name/id and require a y/N confirmation before updating that remote agent (bypassable only with --yes).

rules.section: Action Boundaries → Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Deleting a workspace agent or MCP server (requires y/N confirmation or --yes).

rules.section: Action Boundaries → Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Overwriting an existing project folder during init (requires --force).
Any LLM/model client invoked by the CLI itself (the tool performs no inference).
Any interactive-REPL / coding-agent surface (belongs to deepagents-code).
Any spawner of unauthenticated local dev servers, subprocess shells, hooks, or sandboxes as part of the deploy surface (those belonged to the retired REPL surface).
Folding secret material (e.g. .env contents) into the seeded bundle payload — environment stays environment; it is never merged into the skills, subagent, or managed-directory payload.
Sending email, posting to social/external services, or making outbound calls beyond the deployment platform and its Hub / tracing services.
Letting repository-local deploy state steer authenticated requests (auth resolution ignores repo-local state).
shipping a bundle that failed validation; deploying against an unresolved / uninvocable MCP server; deploying to an unconfirmed agent_id.

rules.section: Known Good Baseline → Normal Restart Cadence
rules.rule_text: Dependencies are expected to be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile.

findings.summary: The CLI enforces TLS on the platform endpoint but never validates that a referenced MCP server URL uses https/wss, contradicting a remit "Never Allowed" boundary.
findings.description: `_normalize_endpoint` scheme-checks only the platform endpoint; the `mcp_server_url` a project references and registers flows through `mcp_resolver._normalize_url` (lowercase/strip only), `project._read_tools_json` (non-empty-string check only), and `mcp-servers add`/`update` with no scheme validation anywhere. A project referencing an `http://` or `ws://` MCP server can therefore be registered and deployed, so the deployed agent's tool traffic and MCP auth headers may travel in cleartext. The remit's Open Questions flag the enforcement point as unconfirmed (possibly platform-side), hence Medium confidence.
policy_rule_text: Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (http:// / ws://) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (https / wss).

evidence.snippet: _normalize_url only strips/lowercases the URL; no https/wss scheme check before it is added to the referenced-server set or resolved for deploy
_execute_mcp_server_add uses urlparse(args.url).hostname only to name the server; --url is accepted and registered with no scheme validation

recommended_actions: Add a scheme check that rejects any `mcp_server_url` whose scheme is not `https`/`wss` at MCP-server registration (`_execute_mcp_server_add`/`update`) and at deploy resolution (`mcp_resolver`), mirroring `_normalize_endpoint`.
If TLS is enforced platform-side, resolve the remit Open Question and document the enforcement point so the guarantee is not silently assumed.

findings.summary: MCP auth secrets are supplied via `--header KEY=VALUE` on the command line, exposing them in the process table and shell history with no environment-indirection option.
findings.description: `mcp-servers add`/`update` take header values as argv, and the documented pattern `--header X-Api-Key=$LANGSMITH_API_KEY` still expands the secret into the process command line. Header values are redacted on `mcp-servers get`, but argv exposure (visible via `ps` to other local users and persisted in shell history) is not mitigated. Low blast radius on a single-user developer machine, but a real credential-handling gap.

evidence.snippet: _parse_header_args reads the raw KEY=VALUE argv entries; the value is taken directly from the command line with no env-var reference option
post-init welcome advertises `--header X-Api-Key=$LANGSMITH_API_KEY`, which the shell expands into the process argv before exec

recommended_actions: Support an indirection form such as `--header-env X-Api-Key=LANGSMITH_API_KEY` (or read header values from a file/stdin) so the secret never appears in argv, and steer the docs toward it.

findings.summary: The package's direct dependencies are floor-pinned (`>=` with upper bounds) in `pyproject.toml` rather than exact-pinned, relying on the committed lockfile for reproducibility.
findings.description: `dependencies` in `pyproject.toml` uses `>=`/`<` ranges (e.g. `httpx>=0.28.1,<1.0.0`, `deepagents>=0.6.12`), so a fresh resolve without the lockfile can pick newer minor/patch versions. A pinned `uv.lock` is committed and Dependabot/CI keep it current, which mitigates the exposure — this is the Medium residual, not a broken control.
policy_rule_text: Dependencies are expected to be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile.

evidence.snippet: dependencies block floor-pins deepagents/langchain/langgraph-sdk/httpx/langsmith/python-dotenv with `>=` (and upper bounds) rather than `==`
committed pinned lockfile provides the exact transitive resolution that the pyproject ranges alone do not

recommended_actions: Keep enforcing installation from the committed `uv.lock` in build/release (already checked by CI); optionally tighten the most security-sensitive direct pins if reproducibility outside the lockfile is required.

findings.summary: The shipped `THREAT_MODEL.md` is auto-generated and stale — its in-scope surface describes a superseded architecture that no longer exists in this deploy-only package.
findings.description: The 59 KB threat model's In-Scope list covers `app.py`, `tools.py`, `mcp_tools.py`, `hooks.py`, sandbox bootstrap, sessions, and server-subprocess management — surfaces removed by the 0.1.0 REPL split; only a top banner flags the staleness. A reviewer relying on the body gets a trust model for a different package, and the current deploy pipeline is not systematically threat-modeled. It is auto-generated and self-described as non-authoritative, so it does not evidence a standing adversarial practice.

evidence.snippet: header banner (2026-05-15) notes it predates the 0.1.0 split; In-Scope list enumerates app.py/tools.py/mcp_tools.py/hooks.py/sandbox/sessions/server modules absent from the current package

recommended_actions: Regenerate or hand-revise `THREAT_MODEL.md` against the current `deepagents_cli.deploy` surface, or replace the stale body with a scoped model so reviewers are not misled.

findings.summary: Mutating actions (create/patch/delete of remote agents and MCP servers) leave no durable audit trail — only stdout summaries and an overwritten last-deploy state file.
findings.description: `_print_deploy_result` writes the deploy summary to stdout and `State.save` persists only the latest agent_id/revision/last_deployed_at (overwritten each run, not append-only); deletions record nothing. The `logging` calls emit through handlers the CLI does not install by default, so nothing is captured. For a tool that mutates shared workspace resources, there is no reconstructable record of what was deployed or deleted, when, or by which run.

evidence.snippet: _print_deploy_result surfaces name/id/revision/URL to stdout only; no durable record of the mutation is written
State.save overwrites a single per-project state file (agent_id/revision/last_deployed_at); history is not retained and deletes are not logged

recommended_actions: Append a structured, timestamped record of each mutating action (deploy/create/patch/delete with agent id, revision, and endpoint) to an append-only log under `~/.deepagents/`, and install a minimal logging handler so the existing `logger` calls are captured.

findings.summary: The repo's root `.mcp.json` wires two remote MCP servers into the developer environment with no client authentication and no tool-definition pinning, outside the remit's declared counterparties.
findings.description: `docs-langchain` and `reference-langchain` are http-transport MCP servers that both use HTTPS (TLS check passes) and carry no secrets (credential check passes), but neither declares client authentication and their tool definitions are unpinned and unsigned. They are read-only public documentation servers used by contributors' editors rather than part of the CLI's runtime or the deployed agent, which bounds the severity to Low; they are nonetheless MCP counterparties not named in the remit.

evidence.snippet: two http-type servers (docs.langchain.com/mcp, reference.langchain.com/mcp) with type/url only — no auth block, no version/integrity pin; both https and secret-free

recommended_actions: Treat the root `.mcp.json` as developer tooling and document it as such; if these servers ever move beyond read-only docs, add authentication and pin/verify their tool definitions.

positives.title: HTTPS-only transport with userinfo and proxy-env rejection
positives.description: `_normalize_endpoint` rejects any non-HTTPS scheme and any embedded userinfo before a request is made, the httpx client runs with `trust_env=False` (ignoring proxy env), and `config.py` blocks a project `.env` from setting endpoint/proxy/TLS variables. Together they harden the one outbound channel against downgrade and redirection.

positives.title: Strict path containment and symlink rejection on all project reads
positives.description: Every project file/dir read passes through `_ensure_project_contained` (resolve-strict plus `relative_to` the root and enclosing container) and an explicit `is_symlink()` rejection, enforcing the tool's bundle-fidelity guarantee that nothing outside the declared project root enters the payload.

positives.title: Confirmation gates for remote-agent updates and resource deletion
positives.description: Deploying to an `agent.json`-declared `agent_id` not previously confirmed prompts `[y/N]` (`_confirm_agent_json_target`), agent and MCP-server deletions prompt before acting, and `--reset` is refused while `agent.json` still declares an `agent_id`.

positives.title: MCP auth-header redaction on read
positives.description: `mcp-servers get` runs the server record through `_redact_mcp_header`, replacing every header `value` with `***` so auth secrets are not printed to the terminal.

positives.title: Committed pinned lockfile with Dependabot and CI enforcement
positives.description: A pinned `uv.lock` is committed for `libs/cli` and kept current by Dependabot (`.github/dependabot.yml`) plus CI lockfile/SDK-pin checks, giving reproducible transitive dependency resolution.

positives.title: Repository-local deploy state ignored for authentication
positives.description: `ApiClient.from_env` resolves auth and endpoint only from the environment, and deploy state is stored under `~/.deepagents/` keyed by endpoint+project-root — repo-local state is intentionally never allowed to steer authenticated requests, closing an attacker-controlled-checkout vector.

positives.title: Targeted security regression tests for trust-boundary controls
positives.description: Unit tests exercise the exact abuse cases the controls defend — symlink rejection at top-level/skill/subagent scopes, non-HTTPS and userinfo endpoint rejection, proxy-env ignore, and header redaction — locking the security behavior in against regression.

log_files.no_logs_note: No physical log files and no default-configured logging sink exist; the CLI is a one-shot local tool that writes user-facing output via print() and emits diagnostics through `logging` handlers it does not install by default, so runtime logs are not persisted — the resulting audit-trail gap is captured as finding PRAX-2026-07-17-005 rather than as inferred log rows.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The CLI has a coherent, code-level safety model across transport, path containment, and approval gates that is stronger than most shipping tools in those specific areas, but it is held below Established overall by a transport-security gap on MCP URLs, minimal continuous monitoring, and a security-testing practice that is regression-test-based rather than a standing adversarial exercise. Zero Trust, Limit Your Domain, Balance, and Supply Chain all land at Established (3) on the strength of operative controls; Build an AI Red Team and Monitor Continuously sit at Partial (2). Nothing here is theater — the gaps are specific and scheduled-review-grade, not systemic absences.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The surface is a closed four-command dispatch table (`init`/`deploy`/`agents`/`mcp-servers`) enforced in code in `main.py`, with the retired interactive REPL genuinely removed (bare invocation redirects to `deepagents-code`) and no model, exec, or subprocess surface; the only drift is documentation referencing a `dev` command that is not wired.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The tool runs no model context, and its data sourcing is tightly controlled — the bundle is a pure function over declared project files with path containment preventing any undeclared or out-of-root content from entering it (`payload.py`, `project.py`); the category is partly not-applicable to a non-LLM tool, hence Medium confidence.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Operative code-level controls interpose on every trust boundary — HTTPS-forced endpoint with userinfo rejection and `trust_env=False` (`api_client.py`), project `.env` blocked from setting endpoint/proxy/TLS vars (`config.py`), symlink and path-escape rejection on all project reads (`project.py`), and confirmation gates for remote-agent updates and deletions; the one gap is that MCP server URLs are never scheme-validated for TLS.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A pinned `uv.lock` is committed and kept current by Dependabot and CI lockfile/SDK-pin checks, and no credentials are present in any workspace file; the direct dependencies in `pyproject.toml` are floor-pinned (`>=` with upper bounds) rather than exact-pinned, which the committed lockfile mitigates.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Targeted security regression tests lock in the trust-boundary controls (symlink rejection at every level, non-HTTPS and userinfo endpoint rejection, proxy-env ignore, header redaction), but there is no evidence of a standing adversarial exercise and the sole threat-model artifact is auto-generated and stale.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Deploy results are surfaced to stdout and a minimal last-deploy state (agent_id/revision/last_deployed_at) is persisted under `~/.deepagents/`, but mutating actions (create/patch/delete of remote agents and MCP servers) leave no durable, append-only audit trail and the `logging` calls have no handlers configured by default.
